en.extract:
The grey-and-buff woodpecker (Hemicircus concretus) is a species of bird in the family Picidae. It is found in Brunei, Indonesia (Sumatra and Borneo), Malaysia, southern Myanmar, and southern Thailand, but has become regionally extinct in Singapore. Its natural habitats are lowland and montane tropical or subtropical moist broadleaf forests.


Taxonomy
The grey-and-buff woodpecker was formally described by the Dutch zoologist Coenraad Jacob Temminck in 1821 and given the binomial name Picus concretus. The type locality is western and central Java. The species is now placed in the genus Hemicircus that was introduced in 1837 by the English naturalist William Swainson.
Two subspecies are recognised:

H. c. sordidus (Eyton, 1845) – Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo and nearby islands
H. c. concretus (Temminck, 1821) – Java
The species is sometimes split with H. concretus as the red-crested woodpecker and H. sordidus as the grey-and-buff woodpecker.


Description

The species has a plump body and short rounded tail, and grows to a length of about 13.5 cm (5.3 in). The head appears large because of the slender neck and the large, cone-shaped crest. The head is largely grey, with a fine white, wavy line running from the cheek to the mantle. In males the forehead is red and the crest grey, while in females, both are grey. Body upper parts and wings are blackish, the feathers being edged with white or buff giving a scalloped effect. Underparts are grey, the tail and underwing dark, and the legs are grey or brown. The beak is long, slender and grey with a black tip, and the iris is chestnut. A gland of unknown function is on the back, the secretions from which sometimes stain the white rump.


Distribution and habitat
This woodpecker is native to tropical southeastern Asia. Its range extends from southern Myanmar and peninsular Thailand and Malaysia, to Sumatra and Borneo. It is a resident species found in lowland and mid-elevation evergreen rainforests, particularly clearings and forest edges, also in plantations, bamboo thickets, cleared areas, wooded urban areas and cultivated land.


Behaviour and ecology
The grey-and-buff woodpecker is usually seen singly or in pairs, but sometimes occurs in mixed species flocks foraging in the canopy. It mainly feeds by gleaning rather than by drilling into the wood, the diet consisting of insects and fruit, including mistletoe (Loranthus) berries. The birds roost communally at night in shallow holes they excavate near each other in dead wood. Nesting takes place in deeper holes or crevices, the breeding season being between December and July.


Status
Although H. concretus occurs in a range of habitats, numbers are thought to be in gradual decline through loss of habitat, and it has become locally extinct in Singapore. It has a very large range and is generally described as being uncommon. The International Union for Conservation of Nature has assessed its conservation status as being of "least concern".


References

The '''grey-and-buff woodpecker''' ('''''Hemicircus concretus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Picidae]]. It is found in [[Brunei]], [[Indonesia]] ([[Sumatra]] and [[Borneo]]), [[Malaysia]], southern [[Myanmar]], and southern [[Thailand]], but has become regionally extinct in [[Singapore]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are lowland and montane [[tropical or subtropical moist broadleaf forest]]s.

==Taxonomy==
The grey-and-buff woodpecker was [[species description|formally described]] by the Dutch zoologist [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck]] in 1821 and given the [[binomial name]] ''Picus concretus''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Temminck | first=Coenraad Jacob  | author-link=Coenraad Jacob Temminck | year=1838 | orig-date=1821 | title=Nouveau recueil de planches coloriées d'oiseaux, pour servir de suite et de complément aux planches enluminées de Buffon | volume=4 | at=Plate 90 | publisher=F.G. Levrault | place=Paris | language=French | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35465119 }} The 5 volumes were originally issued in 102 parts, 1820-1839</ref> The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] is western and central [[Java]].<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1948 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=6 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=223 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14477656 }}</ref> The species is now placed in the genus ''[[Hemicircus]]'' that was introduced in 1837 by the English naturalist [[William Swainson]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Swainson | first=William | author-link=William Swainson | year=1837 | title=On the Natural History and Classification of Birds | volume=2 | place=London  | publisher=John Taylor | page=306 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41945084 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | year=2020 | title=Woodpeckers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/woodpeckers/ | publisher=[[International Ornithologists' Union]] | access-date=15 May 2020 }}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''H. c. sordidus'' ([[Thomas Campbell Eyton|Eyton]], 1845) – [[Malay Peninsula]], [[Sumatra]], [[Borneo]] and nearby islands
* ''H. c. concretus'' (Temminck, 1821) – Java

The species is sometimes split with ''H. concretus'' as the red-crested woodpecker and ''H. sordidus'' as the grey-and-buff woodpecker.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" /><ref>{{cite iucn | author=BirdLife International |url = https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22727212/94943807 | title = ''Hemicircus concretus''| year = 2016 |access-date =15 May 2020}}</ref>

==Description==
[[Image:Grey and buff woodpecker - female.jpg|thumb|left|Female with grey forehead]]
The species has a plump body and short rounded tail, and grows to a length of about {{convert|13.5|cm|in|1|abbr=on}}. The head appears large because of the slender neck and the large, cone-shaped crest. The head is largely grey, with a fine white, wavy line running from the cheek to the mantle. In males the forehead is red and the crest grey, while in females, both are grey. Body upper parts and wings are blackish, the feathers being edged with white or buff giving a scalloped effect. Underparts are grey, the tail and underwing dark, and the legs are grey or brown. The beak is long, slender and grey with a black tip, and the iris is chestnut. A gland of unknown function is on the back, the secretions from which sometimes stain the white rump.<ref name=Gorman/>

==Distribution and habitat==
This woodpecker is native to tropical southeastern Asia. Its range extends from southern Myanmar and peninsular Thailand and Malaysia, to Sumatra and Borneo. It is a resident species found in lowland and mid-elevation evergreen rainforests, particularly clearings and forest edges, also in plantations, bamboo thickets, cleared areas, wooded urban areas and cultivated land.<ref name=Gorman/>

==Behaviour and ecology==
The grey-and-buff woodpecker is usually seen singly or in pairs, but sometimes occurs in mixed species flocks foraging in the canopy. It mainly feeds by gleaning rather than by drilling into the wood, the diet consisting of insects and fruit, including mistletoe (''[[Loranthus]]'') berries.<ref name=bow>{{cite journal | last1=del Hoyo | first1=J. | last2=Winkler | first2=H. | last3=Collar | first3=N. | last4=Kirwan | first4=G.M. | year=2020 | title=Gray-and-buff Woodpecker (''Hemicircus concretus''), version 1.0 | editor1-last=Billerman | editor1-first=S.M. | editor2-last=Keeney | editor2-first=B.K. | editor3-last=Rodewald | editor3-first=P.G. | editor4-last=Schulenberg | editor4-first=T.S. | journal=Birds of the World | location=Ithaca, NY, USA | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | doi=10.2173/bow.gabwoo3.01 }}</ref> The birds roost communally at night in shallow holes they excavate near each other in dead wood.<ref name=Gorman>{{cite book |title=Woodpeckers of the World: A Photographic Guide |last=Gorman |first=Gerard |year=2014 |publisher=Firefly Books |isbn=978-1-77085-309-6 |pages=97–99}}</ref> Nesting takes place in deeper holes or crevices, the breeding season being between December and July.<ref name=bow/>

==Status==
Although ''H. concretus'' occurs in a range of habitats, numbers are thought to be in gradual decline through loss of habitat, and it has become locally extinct in Singapore. It has a very large range and is generally described as being uncommon.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" /> The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] has assessed its conservation status as being of "[[Least-concern species|least concern]]".<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" />

zh.extract:
灰黄啄木鸟（学名：Hemicircus concretus）一种分布于东南亚地区的䴕形目鸟类，隶属于啄木鸟科黑冠啄木鸟属。本种由荷兰动物学家康拉德·雅各·特明克于1821年描述发表。
